title: American Cities by Population
description: Query all columns for all American cities in the CITY table with populations larger than 100000. The CountryCode for America is USA. Order rows by ID ascending.
solution: SELECT * FROM CITY WHERE COUNTRYCODE = 'USA' AND POPULATION > 100000 ORDER BY ID ASC;

title: Delete Duplicate Emails
description: Delete all duplicate emails, keeping only the one with the smallest Id.
solution:
DELETE FROM Person WHERE ROWID NOT IN (SELECT MIN(ROWID) FROM Person GROUP BY Email);
SELECT Id, Email FROM Person ORDER BY Id ASC;

FROM Employee e

title: Type of Triangle
description: Write a query that classifies each row in TRIANGLES as Equilateral, Isosceles, Scalene, or Not A Triangle.
solution:
SELECT CASE
  WHEN A+B<=C OR A+C<=B OR B+C<=A THEN 'Not A Triangle'
  WHEN A=B AND B=C THEN 'Equilateral'
  WHEN A=B OR B=C OR A=C THEN 'Isosceles'
  ELSE 'Scalene'
END FROM TRIANGLES;

title: Weather Observation Station 5
description: Query the two cities with the shortest and longest CITY names. On ties, pick alphabetically first/last.
solution:
SELECT CITY, LENGTH(CITY) FROM STATION
WHERE ROWID IN (SELECT ROWID FROM STATION ORDER BY LENGTH(CITY) ASC, CITY ASC LIMIT 1)
UNION ALL
SELECT CITY, LENGTH(CITY) FROM STATION
WHERE ROWID IN (SELECT ROWID FROM STATION ORDER BY LENGTH(CITY) DESC, CITY DESC LIMIT 1);

title: Cumulative Running Total
description: Calculate a running total of sales amounts ordered by date.
solution:
SELECT sale_date, amount,
  SUM(amount) OVER (ORDER BY sale_date ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW) AS running_total
FROM Sales
ORDER BY sale_date;

title: Pivot Monthly Sales
description: Pivot monthly sales data so each month is a column. Use conditional aggregation.
solution:
SELECT product_id,
  SUM(CASE WHEN CAST(strftime('%m', sale_date) AS INTEGER) = 1 THEN amount ELSE 0 END) AS Jan,
  SUM(CASE WHEN CAST(strftime('%m', sale_date) AS INTEGER) = 2 THEN amount ELSE 0 END) AS Feb,
  SUM(CASE WHEN CAST(strftime('%m', sale_date) AS INTEGER) = 3 THEN amount ELSE 0 END) AS Mar
FROM Sales
GROUP BY product_id
ORDER BY product_id ASC;

title: Basic Common Table Expression (CTE)
description: Use a CTE to find all departments with average salary above the company average.
solution:
WITH dept_avg AS (
  SELECT department_id, AVG(salary) AS avg_sal
  FROM Employees
  GROUP BY department_id
),
company_avg AS (SELECT AVG(salary) AS co_avg FROM Employees)
SELECT department_id, avg_sal
FROM dept_avg, company_avg
WHERE avg_sal > co_avg;

title: Month-over-Month Growth with LAG()
description: Calculate month-over-month revenue growth percentage using the LAG window function.
solution:
SELECT month, revenue,
  LAG(revenue) OVER (ORDER BY month) AS prev_revenue,
  ROUND(100.0 * (revenue - LAG(revenue) OVER (ORDER BY month)) / NULLIF(LAG(revenue) OVER (ORDER BY month), 0), 2) AS growth_pct
FROM MonthlyRevenue ORDER BY month ASC;

title: Recursive CTE for Org Chart
description: Use a recursive CTE to walk an org-chart from CEO down, outputting each employee's level depth.
solution:
WITH RECURSIVE org AS (
  SELECT id, name, manager_id, 0 AS depth FROM Employee WHERE manager_id IS NULL
  UNION ALL
  SELECT e.id, e.name, e.manager_id, o.depth + 1
  FROM Employee e JOIN org o ON e.manager_id = o.id
)
SELECT name, depth FROM org ORDER BY depth ASC, name ASC;

title: Game Play Analysis V — Day-1 Retention
description: For each install date, compute number of installs and Day-1 retention rate.
solution:
SELECT a1.install_dt,
  COUNT(DISTINCT a1.player_id) AS installs,
  ROUND(CAST(COUNT(DISTINCT a2.player_id) AS REAL) / NULLIF(COUNT(DISTINCT a1.player_id), 0), 2) AS Day1_retention
FROM (
  SELECT player_id, MIN(event_date) AS install_dt FROM Activity GROUP BY player_id
) a1
LEFT JOIN Activity a2
  ON a1.player_id = a2.player_id
 AND julianday(a2.event_date) - julianday(a1.install_dt) = 1
GROUP BY a1.install_dt
ORDER BY a1.install_dt ASC;

title: NTH_VALUE and NTILE Analytics
description: Assign quartiles to employees by salary using NTILE(4), and find the 2nd highest salary per department with NTH_VALUE.
solution:
SELECT name, department_id, salary,
  NTILE(4) OVER (ORDER BY salary) AS quartile,
  NTH_VALUE(salary, 2) OVER (
    PARTITION BY department_id
    ORDER BY salary DESC
    ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND UNBOUNDED FOLLOWING
  ) AS second_highest
FROM Employee;

title: Pass Rate Percentages
description: Calculate each student's pass rate (Sum of passes divided by total exams * 100). Use ROUND(value, 2) to fix to 2 decimals. (Cast SUM to FLOAT first). Order by pass_rate descending, then student_id ascending.
solution: SELECT student_id, ROUND(CAST(SUM(passed) AS FLOAT) / COUNT(*) * 100, 2) as pass_rate FROM EXAMS GROUP BY student_id ORDER BY pass_rate DESC, student_id ASC;

title: Positive Account Balances
description: Calculate the net balance (Sum of CREDITs minus Sum of DEBITs) per account. Only return accounts with a balance stringently > 0. Order by balance descending, then account_id ascending.
solution: SELECT account_id, SUM(CASE WHEN type='CREDIT' THEN amount ELSE 0 END) - SUM(CASE WHEN type='DEBIT' THEN amount ELSE 0 END) as balance FROM TRANSACTIONS GROUP BY account_id HAVING balance > 0 ORDER BY balance DESC, account_id ASC;

title: Project Employees III
description: Find the most experienced employee(s) per project.
solution:
SELECT p.project_id, p.employee_id
FROM Project p
JOIN Employee e ON p.employee_id=e.employee_id
WHERE (p.project_id, e.experience_years) IN (
  SELECT p2.project_id, MAX(e2.experience_years)
  FROM Project p2 JOIN Employee e2 ON p2.employee_id=e2.employee_id
  GROUP BY p2.project_id
);

title: Cheapest Project Per Dept
description: Find the department_id, project id, and cost of the cheapest project(s) per department using correlated subqueries. Order by department_id ascending, then project id ascending.
solution: SELECT p1.department_id, p1.id, p1.cost FROM PROJECTS p1 WHERE p1.cost = (SELECT MIN(p2.cost) FROM PROJECTS p2 WHERE p1.department_id = p2.department_id) ORDER BY p1.department_id ASC, p1.id ASC;

title: Elite Sellers

solution: SELECT s.seller_id FROM (SELECT seller_id, SUM(amount) as t FROM SALES GROUP BY seller_id) s WHERE s.t > (SELECT AVG(t) FROM (SELECT SUM(amount) as t FROM SALES GROUP BY seller_id)) ORDER BY s.seller_id ASC;

title: Find the Subtasks That Did Not Execute
description: Find subtasks that were not executed using a recursive CTE.
solution:
WITH RECURSIVE sub AS (
  SELECT task_id, subtasks_count AS sub_id FROM Tasks
  UNION ALL
  SELECT task_id, sub_id - 1 FROM sub WHERE sub_id > 1
)
SELECT s.task_id, s.sub_id AS subtask_id
FROM sub s
LEFT JOIN Executed e ON s.task_id = e.task_id AND s.sub_id = e.subtask_id
WHERE e.subtask_id IS NULL
ORDER BY s.task_id ASC, subtask_id ASC;

title: Hopper Company Queries II
description: For each month, compute the percentage of working drivers who accepted at least one ride.
solution:
WITH months(month) AS (SELECT 1 UNION SELECT 2 UNION SELECT 3 UNION SELECT 4 UNION SELECT 5 UNION SELECT 6 UNION SELECT 7 UNION SELECT 8 UNION SELECT 9 UNION SELECT 10 UNION SELECT 11 UNION SELECT 12),
active AS (
  SELECT driver_id,
         CAST(strftime('%m', join_date) AS INTEGER) AS join_month,
         CAST(strftime('%Y', join_date) AS INTEGER) AS join_year
  FROM Drivers WHERE strftime('%Y', join_date) = '2020'
)
SELECT m.month,
  ROUND(100.0 * COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN r.driver_id IS NOT NULL THEN a.driver_id END) / NULLIF(COUNT(DISTINCT a.driver_id), 0), 2) AS working_percentage
FROM months m
LEFT JOIN active a ON a.join_month <= m.month AND a.join_year = 2020
LEFT JOIN Rides r ON r.driver_id = a.driver_id AND CAST(strftime('%m', r.requested_at) AS INTEGER) = m.month
AND strftime('%Y', r.requested_at) = '2020'
GROUP BY m.month
ORDER BY m.month ASC;

title: Index Strategy and EXPLAIN ANALYZE
description: Identify a missing index for a slow query filtering on (status, created_at). Write the CREATE INDEX statement and interpret EXPLAIN ANALYZE output.
solution:
CREATE INDEX idx_orders_status_created ON orders(status, created_at DESC);
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM orders WHERE status = 'pending' AND created_at >= '2024-01-01';

title: Second Highest Salary
description: Find the second highest distinct salary utilizing a nested subquery (strictly do not use LIMIT or OFFSET).
solution: SELECT MAX(salary) FROM EMPLOYEES WHERE salary < (SELECT MAX(salary) FROM EMPLOYEES);

title: Second Lowest Score
description: Find the second lowest distinct score using a nested subquery (strictly do not use LIMIT or OFFSET).
solution: SELECT MIN(score) FROM SCORES WHERE score > (SELECT MIN(score) FROM SCORES);

title: Table Partitioning by Range
description: Create a partitioned sales table partitioned by year range. Create partitions for 2023 and 2024.
solution:
-- SQLite: logical monthly slice (physical RANGE partitions are unsupported)
SELECT COUNT(*) AS cnt_jan FROM events WHERE event_month = '2024-01';

title: Top Earner Per Dept
description: Find the department_id, name, and salary of the top earner(s) in each department using correlated subqueries (WHERE equals MAX inside same dept). Order by department_id ascending, then name ascending.
solution: SELECT e1.department_id, e1.name, e1.salary FROM EMPLOYEES e1 WHERE e1.salary = (SELECT MAX(e2.salary) FROM EMPLOYEES e2 WHERE e1.department_id = e2.department_id) ORDER BY e1.department_id ASC, e1.name ASC;

title: User Cohort Retention
description: Calculate the overall ratio of users who logged in during a month AND also logged in exactly one month later. (Use strftime('%m', ...) logic bridging l1 and l2). Return the ratio unrounded.
solution: SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT l2.user_id) * 1.0 / COUNT(DISTINCT l1.user_id) as retention_rate FROM LOGINS l1 LEFT JOIN LOGINS l2 ON l1.user_id = l2.user_id AND strftime('%m', l2.login_date) = strftime('%m', date(l1.login_date, '+1 month'));
